[JS Weird World] No.12 How Async Callbacks Actually Work
A few chapters back I talked about how JavaScript runs synchronously. You've definitely heard people throw around the word "async" too. But what does async actually mean?
that at a single point in time, a program that's already running starts running another program
. Sounds like a tongue twister, right? We've already covered that JavaScript runs one line of code at a time, so how on earth does it handle async events?

We already understand the execution stack and execution contexts. When a function finishes running, it leaves the stack (gets released). While JavaScript is waiting around, the JS engine keeps something called the event queue, and the things sitting in there — events, event notifications — are things that might happen soon.
So once the execution stack is completely empty, JavaScript starts paying attention to the event queue. Take a

event, for example. JavaScript watches to see whether it should trigger some function, and if it should, it throws that function onto the execution stack. The moment the event fires, it creates an execution context for that function, the event finishes and gets released, and then it's the next item in the event queue's turn (an HTTP request, say), and so on.
But here's the thing to watch out for —
the event queue only runs when the execution stack is empty
. So is this really async? Nope. It's still synchronous. JavaScript is still running your code one line at a time.
Let's look at the code example from the course below.

. Catch that? JavaScript finishes running the function it's supposed to run before it moves on to the next line of code. So it ends up looking like this.
So if you run this function and then immediately click the page, you'll notice
doesn't show up. It waits until

From this we can see that JavaScript waits for the execution stack to clear before it goes off to handle the next event, and in the meantime it stashes events in the event queue. So how long things take depends on what's sitting in that queue — and that's exactly how JavaScript handles both synchronous and asynchronous work.

A few chapters back I talked about how JavaScript runs synchronously, one line at a time. You've probably also heard the word "async" thrown around a lot. But what does async actually mean? In this post we look at the event queue and how JavaScript handles synchronous and asynchronous work without ever really breaking its one-line-at-a-time rule.
